metadata.summary: Model Context Protocol: open standard (JSON-RPC 2.0) enabling LLMs to access tools, resources, and prompts via a host/client/server architecture.

content.title: Model Context Protocol — Architecture and Core Primitives
content.summary: MCP is an open standard enabling seamless integration between LLM applications and external data sources and tools. It provides a universal interface for reading files, executing functions, and handling contextual prompts via JSON-RPC 2.0 over stateful sessions. Protocol version 2025-11-25. Governance: Linux Foundation (Agentic AI Foundation). Adopters: Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Microsoft, AWS.

why.p: Before MCP, every LLM host (Claude Desktop, VS Code Copilot, custom agents) needed a custom integration per tool. MCP standardizes the interface so one server implementation works across all hosts without modification. The host/client/server separation enforces clean security boundaries: the host enforces consent, clients negotiate capability, and servers remain stateless providers that expose primitives.

list.item: Exposing internal tools (database queries, file system, APIs) to LLM hosts without custom integration per host.
Building reusable tool servers that work across Claude Desktop, VS Code Copilot, and custom agents simultaneously.
Giving agents access to resources that need app-controlled (not model-controlled) presentation of context.
Standardizing tool interfaces across a team — one MCP server, many AI clients.
Enabling sampling: MCP server needs to make LLM calls back through the host (avoids embedding API keys in servers).
Simple single-purpose agent that only needs 1-2 hardcoded tool functions — direct SDK tool definitions are simpler.
Latency-critical paths — MCP stdio transport adds ~5-20ms per round trip; HTTP transport adds network overhead.
Teams without TypeScript or Python experience — MCP SDK requires one of these; no other mature options.
Environments where stdio process management is unreliable (some containerized setups need explicit lifecycle management).
When the tool surface changes frequently — MCP server restarts are required to reload tool definitions in most clients.

section.name: components
section.p: MCP uses a three-tier architecture: Host, Client, and Server.

list.item: Host — Container application (Claude Desktop, IDE). Manages clients, enforces security policies, handles AI/LLM integration.
Client — Connector inside the host. Maintains one server session per server, handles protocol negotiation.
Server — Provider process. Exposes resources, tools, and prompts via MCP primitives. One server per domain of capability.

section.p: Communication: JSON-RPC 2.0, stateful sessions. Transports: stdio (local process), HTTP+SSE, WebSockets, Streamable HTTP.

section.name: primitives
section.p: MCP defines three core primitive types, each with a different control model:

list.item: Tools — Model-controlled functions the LLM can invoke. Examples: API calls, file writes, computations. The model decides when to call them based on the tool description.
Resources — App-controlled contextual data provided to the LLM. Examples: file contents, git history, database records. The host application decides what to expose.
Prompts — User-controlled instruction templates. Examples: slash commands, review workflows. The user selects them from a menu.

example.title: Tool definition (JSON Schema)
example.note: The description is the model's selection signal — make it precise and action-oriented.

section.name: additional-features
section.p: Three server-to-client features extend the base protocol:

list.item: Sampling — Server requests LLM completions from the client host. Avoids embedding API keys in servers. Requires explicit host approval.
Roots — Server inquires about URI and filesystem boundaries the client is willing to expose.
Elicitation — Server requests additional user context mid-session.

section.name: lifecycle
section.p: Every MCP session follows a four-phase lifecycle:

list.item: Initialize — Client sends init request including its capabilities.
Negotiate — Server responds with the subset of capabilities it supports.
Active — Exchange requests, responses, and notifications.
Terminate — Clean session shutdown.

example.title: Capability negotiation message
example.note: listChanged: true means the server will emit notifications when the list changes. Clients that do not support notifications should set this to false.

section.name: sdks
section.p: Official and community SDKs for building MCP servers and clients:

list.item: TypeScript: @modelcontextprotocol/sdk — official; FastMCP wraps it for rapid server development.
Python: mcp — official low-level SDK; FastMCP (pip install fastmcp) provides high-level decorators.
C#: Microsoft.ModelContextProtocol — official.
Go: github.com/mark3labs/mcp-go — community.

section.p: For rapid server development in Python, prefer FastMCP: it handles JSON-RPC boilerplate automatically and exposes tools, resources, and prompts as decorated functions.
